reference: GATE CS — Computer Networks / Network Architecture

map.condition: Packet size given, store-and-forward, k hops
File of M bytes split into packets of p bytes each (p includes header h)
Optimal packet size to minimise total delay
Comparing circuit vs packet: bandwidth utilisation
End-to-end delay for message switching (no pipelining)

formulas.name: Circuit Switching
formulas.latex: \text{Dedicated path reserved end-to-end before data flows.}
formulas.plain: A dedicated physical circuit is established, held for the entire session, then torn down.
formulas.whenToUse: When reasoning about connection setup time, guaranteed bandwidth, or telephone-network comparisons.

terms.means: Call-setup phase before data can be sent
Release phase after the call ends

formulas.trap: GATE often states 'bandwidth reservation' as a property of circuit switching — do not confuse with packet-switching QoS.

formulas.name: Packet Switching
formulas.latex: \text{Data split into packets; each routed independently.}
formulas.plain: No dedicated path; packets share links dynamically (statistical multiplexing).
formulas.whenToUse: Default networking paradigm for the Internet; used in any delay/utilisation computation question.
formulas.trap: Packet switching has MORE delay variation (jitter), not less — a common false statement in GATE.

formulas.name: Message Switching (Store-and-Forward, no fragmentation)
formulas.latex: \text{Entire message stored at each node, then forwarded.}
formulas.plain: The whole file is received at an intermediate node before being forwarded — no pipelining between hops.
formulas.whenToUse: Baseline for comparing against packet switching; used when packet size equals total message size.
formulas.trap: Message switching is NOT the same as packet switching with one large packet when headers must be added at each hop.

formulas.name: Bandwidth Utilisation Comparison
formulas.plain: Circuit: 1/N of capacity used when one of N channels is active. Packet: near full utilisation via multiplexing.
formulas.whenToUse: Questions asking which switching gives better bandwidth utilisation.

terms.means: Number of multiplexed channels on a circuit-switched link

formulas.trap: Packet switching has BETTER utilisation than circuit switching — if a question says otherwise, it is false.

formulas.name: Delay and Jitter Comparison
formulas.plain: Circuit switching: fixed delay, zero jitter. Packet switching: variable queuing delay, non-zero jitter.
formulas.whenToUse: True/false questions about delay variation or which switching suits real-time applications.
formulas.trap: A classic GATE false statement is 'packet switching results in less variation in delay than circuit switching' — this is FALSE.

formulas.name: Connection Setup Overhead
formulas.plain: Total time = setup + data transfer + teardown; packet switching has no setup phase.
formulas.whenToUse: When comparing total transfer time including call setup, especially for short messages.
formulas.trap: For very short messages, circuit switching may be slower overall due to setup overhead even though per-bit transmission delay is fixed.

formulas.name: Single-Packet End-to-End Delay (k hops)
formulas.plain: T = k * (L / B)  where k = number of links, L = packet size (bytes), B = link bandwidth (bytes/s).
formulas.whenToUse: When a single packet traverses k store-and-forward links of equal bandwidth.
formulas.trap: Do not confuse number of routers with number of links: k links = k-1 intermediate routers + 2 endpoints.

formulas.name: Pipelined Multi-Packet End-to-End Delay
formulas.plain: T = (n_pkt + k - 1) * (L / B)  — pipelining lets last packet overlap with earlier ones.
formulas.whenToUse: When a file is split into n_pkt equal packets, each of size L bytes, sent over k links.

terms.means: Number of packets the file is split into

formulas.trap: Propagation delay is often zero in GATE store-and-forward problems unless explicitly given — check the question.

formulas.name: Pipelined Delay with Header Overhead
formulas.plain: n_pkt = ceil(M / (p-h));  T = (n_pkt + k - 1) * p / B  where p = total packet size, h = header bytes.
formulas.whenToUse: File of M data bytes, packets of total size p (payload = p-h), sent over k store-and-forward links.
formulas.trap: Payload per packet is (p - h), NOT p — forgetting header overhead inflates n_pkt.

formulas.name: Message Switching Delay (no fragmentation)
formulas.plain: T_msg = k * M / B — entire message transmitted at each hop before forwarding.
formulas.whenToUse: Baseline comparison: packet switching is faster due to pipelining whenever k > 1.
formulas.trap: Message switching delay is k times the single-link delay — packet switching with pipelining beats this for k > 1.

formulas.name: Total Delay as Function of Packet Size
formulas.plain: T(p) = (1/B) * [ M + M*h/(p-h) + (k-1)*p ]  — sum of data bits + header overhead + pipeline tail.
formulas.whenToUse: Setting up the optimisation to find optimal packet size p that minimises total transmission time.

terms.means: Total packet size (bytes), the variable to optimise
Total data bytes in the file

formulas.trap: The term (k-1)*p accounts for the pipeline tail at k-1 intermediate nodes — often omitted in student derivations.

formulas.name: Optimal Packet Size (minimises total delay)
formulas.plain: p* = h + sqrt(M*h / (k-1))  — derived by dT/dp = 0.
formulas.whenToUse: GATE numerical question: given M, h, k, find the packet size that minimises end-to-end delay.

terms.means: Optimal total packet size in bytes

formulas.trap: k is the number of LINKS, not routers. For 2 intermediate nodes, k = 3 links.

formulas.name: Optimal Payload Size
formulas.whenToUse: When the question asks for optimal payload (data portion) rather than total packet size.
formulas.trap: Do not substitute payload for p in the formula — p is the full packet (header + payload).

formulas.name: TDM Circuit Switching — Number of Channels
formulas.plain: N = B_link / B_channel — total link bandwidth divided evenly among N circuit channels.
formulas.whenToUse: Computing how many simultaneous circuit-switched calls a link can support.
formulas.trap: Each channel always holds its allocation even during silence — this is the waste that packet switching avoids.

formulas.name: Statistical Multiplexing Gain (Packet Switching)
formulas.plain: Packet switching supports many more users than N when each user is active only a fraction of the time.
formulas.whenToUse: Qualitative questions about why packet switching supports more simultaneous users than circuit switching.

terms.means: Probability a user is actively transmitting
Max simultaneous circuit-switched users on same link

formulas.trap: The gain only applies when users are bursty — for continuous streams, packet and circuit switching support the same number.
